topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The petals were an offensively bright, saccharine yellow, clustered around a stalk that looked suspiciously limp. I stared at them on the kitchen counter, the sheer artificiality of the arrangement making my stomach clench. There was no note, just the cheap plastic wrapping that felt offensively flimsy against my fingertips. I picked up a stem, the scent—a cloying, overly sweet perfume—hit me like a physical assault. I dropped it back onto the granite with a sharp clatter. Why? Who sends wilted garbage wrapped in cellophane? I scrubbed my hands vigorously at the sink, trying to wash away the lingering impression of that saccharine intrusion.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed over the bouquet, I didn't even bother to look at the sender's face. My eyes flicked only to the arrangement itself: drooping roses, the kind you buy from a discount kiosk. It was all too much effort, too much saccharine gesture for a gesture devoid of context. I took the box with two fingers, maneuvering it away from the door, and carried it straight to the recycling bin. The weight of the paper and plastic felt heavy, an unwelcome burden. I didn't want to acknowledge the sender; acknowledging them would imply some sort of significance to this clumsy offering.

[story 3]

I found the flowers sitting on my desk, abandoned amidst stacks of overdue bills. They were an alarming shade of pink, the edges of the petals slightly bruised. I leaned in, inhaling deeply, and a tightness began to constrict in my chest. It smelled overwhelmingly of cheap chemicals and desperate sentimentality. I picked up a single bloom, turning it over slowly, examining the imperfect texture with a profound sense of alienation. My jaw tightened, and I slowly placed the flower back down, not caring where, just wanting the object to cease existing in my immediate vicinity.
